questions.question: What is the difference between this lesson's movement practices and dance/movement therapy?

options.text: There is no difference—both are the same thing
This lesson teaches accessible movement for emotional awareness/regulation; dance/movement therapy is clinical treatment by credentialed therapists
Dance/movement therapy is just for professional dancers
This lesson is more effective than clinical dance/movement therapy

questions.explanation: Dance/movement therapy (DMT) is a clinical treatment modality delivered by credentialed therapists (R-DMT, BC-DMT) for trauma, eating disorders, psychosis, and other clinical conditions. This lesson teaches accessible movement practices for emotional awareness and regulation that anyone can use at home without training. If you need clinical support, work with a licensed dance/movement therapist.

questions.question: How should you adapt rhythmic movement based on your nervous system state?

options.text: Always use the same rhythm regardless of your state
Hyperaroused (anxious): slow rhythm to calm; Hypoaroused (shut down): moderate rhythm to activate; High activation: fast rhythm to discharge
Never use movement when dysregulated—only when already calm
Fast movement is always best for all emotional states

questions.explanation: Adapt rhythm to your state: Hyperaroused/anxious → slow rhythms (swaying, slow walking) to downregulate; Hypoaroused/shut down → moderate rhythms (brisk walking, bouncing) to gently activate; High activation needing discharge → fast rhythms (dancing, shaking) to release energy. Your nervous system entrains to external rhythm, so matching rhythm to regulation goal is key.

questions.question: Why does bilateral movement (crossing midline, using both sides) support emotional regulation?

options.text: It burns more calories than single-sided movement
Bilateral activation engages both brain hemispheres and supports integration, similar to bilateral drawing or EMDR
Bilateral movement is just a trendy technique with no real benefits
It only works for professional athletes

questions.explanation: Bilateral movement (crossing midline, alternating sides, or moving both sides simultaneously) engages both brain hemispheres and may support integration of cognitive and emotional processing. This is similar to the mechanisms in bilateral drawing, EMDR, or bilateral tapping—coordinating both sides creates organizing effect and supports regulation.

questions.question: What should you do if you have limited mobility or chronic pain but want to use movement for regulation?

options.text: Movement practices aren't for you—skip this entirely
Adapt with seated movement (upper body gestures), minimal movement (micro-movements, breath with visualization), or non-standing options
Force yourself to do full-body standing movement even if painful
Wait until you're completely healed before trying any movement

questions.explanation: Movement practices can be adapted for all ability levels: seated movement (upper body, rocking in chair, hand dancing), minimal movement (micro-gestures, imagined movement, muscle tensing/releasing), or lying-down options. Even small movements provide sensory feedback and can support regulation. Work within your capacity—movement doesn't require standing or bearing weight.

questions.question: When is self-guided movement NOT appropriate and professional support is needed?

options.text: Whenever you feel self-conscious about moving
If you're experiencing severe eating disorders, trauma responses activated by movement, or dissociation triggered by embodied practices
If you don't have dance training or natural rhythm
Self-guided movement is always sufficient—professional help is never needed

questions.explanation: Self-guided movement is not appropriate for severe eating disorders, body dysmorphia, trauma responses activated by embodied work, or dissociation triggered by movement practices. These conditions require specialized support from licensed dance/movement therapists or trauma-informed therapists. Self-guided movement complements clinical care but cannot replace it for clinical-level conditions.
